en.extract:
The rock wren (Salpinctes obsoletus) is a small songbird of the wren family native to the rocky areas of western North America. There are 8 subspecies of the rock wren, one of which is extinct. They are similar in size to other wrens, with a grey-brown coat, a brown rump, and a speckled white throat. They are visually similar to the canyon wren, which they share a habitat with. They are noted for their variable song and song pattern, used for territory defense, as well as their habit of building "pavements" with flat rocks around their nest to help nestlings stay dry. Rock wrens are serially monogamous, forming pairs for nesting season. Nestbuilding usually begins in March, usually in crevices, and the first brood are laid late April to June, with a possible second brood later in June. They are largely terrestrial foragers, rarely flying. Their diet consists of insects, mostly weevils, during the spring and summer, shifting to grain during autumn and winter. Though they are a least-concern species, their population size has declined by 13% from 2010 to 2020.


Taxonomy
The rock wren was first described by Thomas Say, published in Edwin James' book Account of an Expedition from Pittsburgh to the Rocky Mountains, as Troglodytes obsoleta. The work was available in 1822, even though the date printed on the title page is 1823; 1822 thus counts as the date of description of the species. It was later moved to its own monotypic genus, Salpinctes, by Jean Cabanis in 1847, because of its straighter tail. Salpinctes formerly included the canyon wren (synonym Thryothorus mexicanus), but it was later removed.
The genus name, Salpinctes, comes from the Greek word for 'trumpeter'. Its specific epithet, obsoletus, is Latin for 'worn out'. There are 8 subspecies, split into 2 groups (obsoletus and guttatus).


Northern group (obsoletus)
S. o. obsoletus (Say, 1822)—Nominate subspecies.
S. o. guadeloupensis (Ridgway, 1876)—Darker than other island populations, with a longer, heavier bill and shorter wing and tail. Juveniles have barred, dusky backs. Its name, guadeloupensis, comes from its habitat, Guadalupe Island, off western Baja California.
S. o. tenuirostris (van Rossem, 1943)—Similar to nominate, but with thinner and longer bill. Found on San Benito Island. Its name comes from the Latin words tenuis and rostrum, meaning 'slender' and 'beak', respectively.
S. o. exsul (Ridgway, 1903)—Extinct from the eruption of Volcán Bárcena. Formerly found on San Benedicto Island. Said to have been generally darker than nominate, along with more yellowish-brown stripes on outer tail feathers. Its name comes from the Latin word exsul, meaning 'exile'.
S. o. neglectus (Nelson, 1897)—Similar to nominate, but with barred flanks, darker, and more spots on the breast. Noted to be variable in appearance. Found in the highlands of southern Mexico. Name comes from Latin neglectus, meaning 'overlooked'.


Southern group (guttatus)
S. o. guttatus (Salvin and Godman, 1891)—Flanks are spotted, instead of barred like fasciatus. Found in the highlands of El Salvador. Name comes from Latin guttatus, meaning 'spotted'.
S. o. fasciatus (Salvin and Godman, 1891)—Flanks are barred, instead of spotted like guttatus. Found in the highlands of Nicaragua. Name comes from Latin fasciatus, meaning 'banded'.
S. o. costaricensis (van Rossem, 1941)—Generally paler compared to fasciatus, as well as having a longer, larger bill. Found in the highlands of northwestern Costa Rica. The name comes from the name for Costa Rica.


Description
The rock wren is between 12.5–15 cm (4.9–5.9 in) in length and has a wingspan of 22–24 cm (8.7–9.4 in). It has a bill length of 18.7 mm (0.74 in) in males and 17.8 mm (0.70 in) in females. Its wing length is 70.3 mm (2.77 in) in males, and slightly less at 67.8 mm (2.67 in) in females; similarly, its tail length is 51.9 mm (2.04 in) and 47.8 mm (1.88 in) in males and females, respectively. However, males weigh 15.7 g (0.55 oz), slightly less than females, who weigh 17.2 g (0.61 oz).
Rock wrens have grey-brown upperparts with small black and white spots and pale grey underparts with a light brown rump. Additional features include a light grey line extending through the eye, a long and sharp bill, a speckled light gray throat, a long barred tail, zebra-striped undertail coverts, and dark legs. The juvenile plumage is similar to that of an adult, except for a slightly more vibrant buff, similar to a washed-out cinnamon color, fluffier body feathers, and the absence of a dark streak and spots on the underparts.
Though they are very distinct in their habitat, it is possible to confuse them with the canyon wren, which have superficially similar coloring; however, the canyon wren has solid white throats and generally brighter, less muddled colors.


Distribution and habitat
Rock wrens are permanent residents in the south of their range, including California; western Washington; northern Nevada, Arizona, and New Mexico; southern Guatemala and Nicaragua; northwestern Costa Rica and Panama; and inland Mexico, including Baja California. They are also residents of Guadalupe Island and San Benito Island. They are occasional vagrants in the eastern United States. During the breeding season, their range extends to the southern interior of British Columbia, as well as Wyoming, Montana, Idaho, and Utah. They are commonly found in rocky open habitats, such as exposed rock, canyons, cliffs; however, rock is not a firm requirement, as they can also be found in large eroded sandbanks. Rock wrens are rare in urban areas, instead preferring rocky areas with at least some steep features, enough shrubbery to forage in, and ample crevices for nesting. The rock wren is very tolerant of altitudes, breeding from 60 m (200 ft) to 3,600 m (11,800 ft) above sea level; however, they are most common below 2,000 m (6,600 ft).


Behavior
Rock wrens are serially monogamous. During nesting season, males are territorial, guarding their area of around 1.8 hectares (4.4 acres) in size with songs, calls, and threat displays, such as bobbing. Males have been observed courting by bringing food to females.


Breeding
Nest-building begins in March, and the first brood occurs late April to June, and a second brood can occur from mid-June to early August, with up to 3 broods per year. Rock wrens nest on stone surfaces in crevices, cavities, or other similar sheltered areas. They lay 5–6 eggs per clutch, with incubation being around 14–16 days. Incubation is handled by the female alone; however, males will occasionally bring food.
Eggs are ovate, usually 18.6 mm × 14.8 mm (0.73 in × 0.58 in) in size and around 2.27 g (0.080 oz) in mass. One egg is laid per day.

The outsides of the nests are roughly 8.9 cm (3.5 in) in diameter and 3.4 cm (1.3 in) in depth, with the inside 6.7 cm (2.6 in) and 2.9 cm (1.1 in) in diameter and depth, respectively. Nests are usually built in cavities with openings around 2 times the bird's height, or 10.2 cm (4.0 in). They are built on top of a stone foundation, and consist of stones, plant material, trash, and other debris. Nests often include a "pavement" made with stones leading up to the nest to keep the nest and nestlings dry, helping them maintain their body temperature. Pavements consist of flat stones, on average 3.1 g (0.11 oz) in weight, 27 by 18 mm (1.06 by 0.71 in) in width and length, and 5 mm (0.20 in) thick. These stones weigh on average 20% of the bird's body mass, with an average of 230 stones per nest; the amount of stones vary with the size of the nest cavity, with larger cavities having more stones.
Nestlings leave the nest around 14–16 days old, moving to a different rock within 75 m (246 ft) from the nest, continuing to receive care from parents for about 1 week, and remained on the parent's territory for 4 more weeks. Nestlings molted at 28–35 days, however, they still possessed gape flanges.


Feeding

Rock wrens are largely terrestrial, only flying for short durations, and instead using a very rapid gait to move around. They eat insects, such as leafhoppers, ants, and weevils; occasionally, other arthropods like spiders; and, rarely, seeds. Rock wrens mainly forage during the day on open ground, rummaging the surface of rocks and soil and probing crevices.
Nestlings were observed being fed grasshoppers, along with other insects; however, large portions of the prey could not be identified due to their small size.


Vocalizations

Female rock wrens do not sing; however, males have large repertoires of 69–119 syllables, each song a 1–2.5 second trill of repeated syllables, with 3–5 seconds between songs. Rock wrens have five call types: a two-part tic-ear/spit-tee sometimes used to warn of intruders; a rough trill, sometimes following a spit, used as an alarm; an agitated chatter; and an aggressive k'chee used during confrontations.
Rock wrens sing with a distinctive pattern intermediate between "eventual variety" (repeating one syllable before switching) and "immediate variety" (never repeating). A male typically sings each syllable three to four times within a burst of 10–15 syllables, rarely repeating the same syllable consecutively. Song is primarily used for territorial defense, with males having greater repertoire size and pitch variety being more effective at deterring rivals.


Conservation
The rock wren is a least-concern species on the IUCN Red List, with an estimated population of 3.4 million in the United States and Canada, with the global population being around 4.1 million. However, the population is slowly declining, with a 13% decrease in population from 2010 to 2020. The cause of this is unknown. Little is known about the causes of mortality or lifespan in adults, but nests are subject to predation. Observed nest predators included the striped racer snake, the desert woodrat, and the white-tailed antelope squirrel, as well as bullsnakes and prairie rattlesnakes. Brown-headed cowbirds are brood parasites of this species.


See also
Volcán Bárcena, the volcano that caused the San Benedicto rock wren (a subspecies of Salpinctes) to go extinct in 1952


References


External links

Rock Wren by John Audubon
Rock Wren - Cornell Lab of Ornithology
Rock Wren - eNature.com; Archived 2016-08-02 at the Wayback Machine
Rock Wren - USGS Patuxent Bird Information
Rock Wren photo gallery; Archived 2009-07-03 at the Wayback Machine Vireo

The '''rock wren''' ('''''Salpinctes obsoletus''''') is a small [[songbird]] of the [[wren]] family native to the rocky areas of western [[North America]]. There are 8 subspecies of the rock wren, one of which is extinct. They are similar in size to other wrens, with a grey-brown coat, a brown rump, and a speckled white throat. They are visually similar to the [[canyon wren]], which they share a habitat with. They are noted for their variable [[Bird vocalization|song]] and song pattern, used for [[Territory (animal)|territory]] defense, as well as their habit of building "pavements" with flat rocks around their nest to help nestlings stay dry. Rock wrens are [[serially monogamous]], forming pairs for nesting season. Nestbuilding usually begins in March, usually in crevices, and the first brood are laid late April to June, with a possible second brood later in June. They are largely terrestrial foragers, rarely flying. Their diet consists of [[insect]]s, mostly [[weevil]]s, during the spring and summer, shifting to [[grain]] during autumn and winter. Though they are a [[least-concern species]], their population size has declined by 13% from 2010 to 2020.

== Taxonomy<!-- done --> ==
The rock wren was first described by [[Thomas Say]], published in [[Edwin James (scientist)|Edwin James]]' book ''Account of an Expedition from Pittsburgh to the Rocky Mountains'', as ''Troglodytes obsoleta''.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=James |first1=Edwin |title=Account of an expedition from Pittsburgh to the Rocky Mountains, performed in the years 1819 and '20, by order of the Hon. J. C. Calhoun, sec'y of war: under the command of Major Stephen H. Long. From the notes of Major Long, Mr. T. Say, and other gentlemen of the party |orig-date=1822 |date=1823 |publisher=H.C. Carey & I. Lea |volume=2 |location=Philadelphia |page=4 |url= https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40216950|quote=Genus Troglodytes, Cuvier — T. ''obsoleta'', Say. Above dusky-brownish, slightly undulated with pale, tinted with dull ferruginous on the top of the head and superior portion of the back; sides of the head dull whitish, with a broad brown line passing through the eye to the origin of the neck; primaries plain, being entirely destitute of undulations or spots; tail coverts pale, each with four or five fuscous bands; chin, neck beneath, and breast whitish, each feather marked by a longitudinal line of light brown; belly white; flanks a little tinged with ferruginous; inferior tail coverts white, each feather bifasciate with black-brown; tail simple, broadly tipped with ferruginous-yellow, and with black before the tip, the remaining portion color of the wings, and obsoletely banded; these bands are more distinct on the two middle feathers, which are destitute of the black and yellowish termination; exterior plume marked by four yellowish white spots on the exterior web, and by two larger ones on the inner web; the tip is dusky, length six inches; bill, from the corner of the mouth, rather more than one inch.<!-- can't spank me, its public domain -->}}</ref> The work was available in 1822, even though the date printed on the title page is 1823; 1822 thus counts as the date of description of the species.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Woodman |first1=N. |title=History and dating of the publication of the Philadelphia (1822) and London (1823) editions of Edwin James's Account of an expedition from Pittsburgh to the Rocky Mountains |journal=Archives of Natural History |date=2010 |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=28–38 |doi=10.3366/E0260954109001636}}</ref> It was later moved to its own monotypic genus, ''Salpinctes'', by [[Jean Cabanis]] in 1847, because of its straighter tail.<ref name=":6">{{Cite book |last=Cabanis |first=Jean Louis |url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/_/2h1FAQAAMAAJ?hl=en&gbpv=0 |title=Ornithologische Notizen: I und II |date=1847 |language=de}}</ref> ''Salpinctes'' formerly included the [[canyon wren]] (synonym ''Thryothorus mexicanus''), but it was later removed.<ref name=":6" />

The genus name, ''Salpinctes'', comes from the [[Greek language|Greek]] word for 'trumpeter'. Its specific epithet, ''obsoletus'', is Latin for 'worn out'. There are 8 subspecies, split into 2 groups (''obsoletus'' and ''guttatus'').<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last1=Benedict |first1=Lauryn |last2=Warning |first2=Nathanial B. |last3=Najar |first3=Nadje A. |last4=Pitt |first4=Stephanie G. |last5=Lowther |first5=Peter E. |last6=Kroodsma |first6=Donald E. |last7=Farley |first7=Greg H. |date=2021 |title=Rock Wren (Salpinctes obsoletus), version 2.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/rocwre/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.rocwre.02 |issn=2771-3105 |url-access=subscription}}</ref>

=== Northern group (''obsoletus'') ===
{{Subspecies bullet list item|scientific name=S. o. obsoletus|describer name=Thomas Say|describer last name=Say|year described=1822|description=Nominate subspecies.<ref name=":0"/>}}{{Subspecies bullet list item|scientific name=S. o. guadeloupensis|describer last name=Ridgway|describer name=Robert Ridgway|year described=1876|description=Darker than other island populations, with a longer, heavier bill and shorter wing and tail. Juveniles have barred, dusky backs. Its name, ''guadeloupensis'', comes from its habitat, [[Guadalupe Island]], off western [[Baja California]].<ref name=":0"/>}}{{Subspecies bullet list item|scientific name=S. o. tenuirostris|describer name=Adriaan Joseph van Rossem|describer last name=van Rossem|year described=1943|description=Similar to nominate, but with thinner and longer bill. Found on [[San Benito Island]]. Its name comes from the Latin words ''tenuis'' and ''rostrum'', meaning 'slender' and 'beak', respectively.<ref name=":0"/>}}{{Subspecies bullet list item|scientific name=S. o. exsul|describer last name=Ridgway|year described=1903|description=Extinct from the eruption of [[Volcán Bárcena]]. Formerly found on [[San Benedicto Island]]. Said to have been generally darker than nominate, along with more yellowish-brown stripes on outer [[rectrices|tail feathers]]. Its name comes from the Latin word ''exsul'', meaning 'exile'.<ref name=":0"/> <!-- "cinnamon-buff bars on outer rectrices" is taken word for word from BOTW, don't really know how to rephrase it-->|describer name=Robert Ridgway}}{{Subspecies bullet list item|scientific name=S. o. neglectus|describer name=Edward William Nelson|describer last name=Nelson|year described=1897|description=Similar to nominate, but with barred flanks, darker, and more spots on the breast. Noted to be variable in appearance. Found in the highlands of southern [[Mexico]]. Name comes from Latin ''neglectus'', meaning 'overlooked'.<ref name=":0"/>}}

=== Southern group (''guttatus'') ===
{{Subspecies bullet list item|scientific name=S. o. guttatus|describer last name=Salvin and Godman|year described=1891|description=Flanks are spotted, instead of barred like ''fasciatus''. Found in the highlands of [[El Salvador]]. Name comes from Latin ''guttatus'', meaning 'spotted'.<ref name=":0"/>|describer name=Osbert Salvin}}{{Subspecies bullet list item|scientific name=S. o. fasciatus|describer last name=Salvin and Godman|year described=1891|description=Flanks are barred, instead of spotted like ''guttatus''. Found in the highlands of [[Nicaragua]]. Name comes from Latin ''fasciatus'', meaning 'banded'.<ref name=":0"/>|describer name=Osbert Salvin}}{{Subspecies bullet list item|scientific name=S. o. costaricensis|describer last name=van Rossem|year described=1941|description=Generally paler compared to ''fasciatus'', as well as having a longer, larger bill. Found in the highlands of northwestern Costa Rica. The name comes from the name for [[Costa Rica]].<ref name=":0"/>|describer name=Adriaan Joseph van Rossem}}

== Description<!-- done --> ==
The rock wren is between {{Convert|12.5-15|cm|in|abbr=on}} in length and has a [[wingspan]] of {{Convert|22-24|cm|in|abbr=on}}. It has a bill length of {{Convert|18.7|mm|in|abbr=on}} in males and {{Convert|17.8|mm|in|abbr=on}} in females. Its wing length is {{Convert|70.3|mm|in|abbr=on}} in males, and slightly less at {{Convert|67.8|mm|in|abbr=on}} in females; similarly, its tail length is {{Convert|51.9|mm|in|abbr=on}} and {{Convert|47.8|mm|in|abbr=on}} in males and females, respectively. However, males weigh {{Convert|15.7|g|oz|abbr=on}}, slightly less than females, who weigh {{Convert|17.2|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book |title=CRC handbook of avian body masses |date=2008 |publisher=CRC Press |isbn=978-1-4200-6444-5 |editor-last=Dunning |editor-first=John B. |edition=2nd |location=Boca Raton}}</ref>

Rock wrens have grey-brown upperparts with small black and white spots and pale grey underparts with a light brown rump.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=Rock Wren Life History, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology |url=https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Rock_Wren/lifehistory |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.allaboutbirds.org |language=en}}</ref> Additional features include a light grey line extending through the eye, a long and sharp [[beak|bill]],<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |date=2024-03-14 |title=Bird of the Month: Rock Wren (Salpinctes obsoletus) |url=https://southwest.audubon.org/news/bird-month-rock-wren-salpinctes-obsoletus |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=Audubon Southwest |language=en}}</ref> a speckled light gray throat, a long barred tail, zebra-striped undertail coverts, and dark legs.<ref name=":0" /> The juvenile plumage is similar to that of an adult, except for a slightly more vibrant buff, similar to a washed-out cinnamon color, fluffier body feathers, and the absence of a dark streak and spots on the underparts.<ref name=":0" />

Though they are very distinct in their habitat, it is possible to confuse them with the [[canyon wren]], which have superficially similar coloring; however, the canyon wren has solid white throats and generally brighter, less muddled colors.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

== Distribution and habitat ==
Rock wrens are permanent residents in the south of their range, including [[California]]; western [[Washington (state)|Washington]]; northern [[Nevada]], [[Arizona]], and [[New Mexico]]; southern [[Guatemala]] and [[Nicaragua]]; northwestern [[Costa Rica]] and [[Panama]]; and inland [[Mexico]], including [[Baja California]].<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=Rock Wren {{!}} Audubon Field Guide |url=https://www.audubon.org/field-guide/bird/rock-wren |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.audubon.org |language=en |quote=Strongly migratory, departing from northern part of range for the winter. Strays sometimes wander east in fall, and have even reached the Atlantic Coast.}}</ref> They are also residents of [[Guadalupe Island|Guadalupe]] [[San Benito Island|Island]] and [[San Benito Island]].<ref name=":0" /> They are occasional vagrants in the eastern United States. During the breeding season, their range extends to the southern interior of [[British Columbia]], as well as [[Wyoming]], [[Montana]], [[Idaho]], and [[Utah]].<ref name=":4" /> They are commonly found in rocky open habitats, such as exposed rock, canyons, cliffs; however, rock is not a firm requirement, as they can also be found in large eroded sandbanks.<ref name=":0" /> Rock wrens are rare in urban areas, instead preferring rocky areas with at least some steep features, enough shrubbery to forage in, and ample crevices for nesting.<ref name=":0" /> The rock wren is very tolerant of altitudes, breeding from {{Convert|60|m|ft|abbr=on}} to {{Convert|3600|m|ft|abbr=on}} above sea level;<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Patten |first=Michael A. |date=1995 |title=Status and Distribution of California Birds California Birds: Their Status and Distribution Arnold Small |url=https://academic.oup.com/condor/article/97/2/608-611/5126197 |journal=The Condor |volume=97 |issue=2 |pages=608–611 |doi=10.2307/1369052 |issn=1938-5129}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Smith |first=Michael Roger |url=https://books.google.com/books/about/Breeding_Birds_of_Washington_State.html?id=_epFAAAAYAAJ |title=Breeding Birds of Washington State: Location Data and Predicted Distributions |last2=Mattocks |first2=Philip Ward |last3=Cassidy |first3=Kelly Michela |date=1997 |publisher=Seattle Audubon Society |isbn=978-0-914516-09-5 |language=en}}</ref> however, they are most common below {{Convert|2000|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":0" />

== Behavior ==
Rock wrens are [[serially monogamous]]. During nesting season, males are territorial, guarding their area of around {{Convert|1.8|ha|acre}} in size with songs, calls, and threat displays, such as bobbing. Males have been observed [[Courtship display|courting]] by bringing food to females.<ref name=":0" />

=== Breeding<!-- done --> ===
Nest-building begins in March, and the first brood occurs late April to June, and a second brood can occur from mid-June to early August, with up to 3 broods per year. Rock wrens nest on stone surfaces in crevices, cavities, or other similar sheltered areas. They lay 5–6 [[Bird egg|eggs]] per [[Clutch (eggs)|clutch]], with [[Egg incubation|incubation]] being around 14{{En dash}}16 days.<ref name=":3" /> Incubation is handled by the female alone; however, males will occasionally bring food.<ref name=":0" />

Eggs are ovate, usually {{Convert|18.6x14.8|mm|in|abbr=on}} in size and around {{Convert|2.27|g|oz|abbr=on}} in mass.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Bent |first=Arthur |date=1948-01-01 |title=Life Histories of North American Nuthatches, Wrens, Thrashers And Their Allies: Order Passeriformes |url=https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/biosciornithology/99 |journal=Papers in Ornithology}}</ref> One egg is laid per day.<ref name=":0" />
[[File:Fledgling Rock Wren (51379673210).jpg|thumb|A fledgling rock wren]]
The outsides of the nests are roughly {{Convert|8.9|cm|in|abbr=on}} in diameter and {{Convert|3.4|cm|in|abbr=on}} in depth, with the inside {{Convert|6.7|cm|in|abbr=on}} and {{Convert|2.9|cm|in|abbr=on}} in diameter and depth, respectively.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Harrison |first=Hal H. |title=A field guide to western birds' nests: of 520 species found breeding in the U. S. west of the Mississippi River |date=1979 |publisher=Houghton Mifflin |others=Mada Harrison |isbn=978-0-395-27629-7 |series=The Peterson field guide series |location=Boston}}</ref> Nests are usually built in cavities with openings around 2 times the bird's height, or {{Convert|10.2|cm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":0" /> They are built on top of a stone foundation, and consist of stones, plant material, trash, and other debris.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=Bailey |first=Florence |date=1904-05-01 |title=Twelve Rock Wren Nests in New Mexico |url=https://digitalcommons.usf.edu/condor/vol6/iss3/3 |journal=Condor |volume=6 |issue=3 |pages=68–70 |doi=10.2307/1361099 |jstor=1361099 }}</ref> Nests often include a "pavement" made with stones leading up to the nest to keep the nest and nestlings dry, helping them maintain their body temperature.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Warning |first1=Nathanial |last2=Benedict |first2=Lauryn |date=January 2015 |title=Paving the way: Multifunctional nest architecture of the Rock Wren |url=https://academic.oup.com/auk/article/132/1/288/5149195 |journal=The Auk |language=en |volume=132 |issue=1 |pages=288–299 |doi=10.1642/AUK-14-186.1 |issn=0004-8038 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220302113759/https://academic.oup.com/auk/article/132/1/288/5149195 |archive-date=2022-03-02 |access-date=2026-01-04 |url-status=live }}</ref> Pavements consist of flat stones, on average {{Convert|3.1|g|oz|abbr=on}} in weight, {{Convert|27 by 18|mm|in|abbr=on}} in width and length, and {{Convert|5|mm|in|abbr=on}} thick. These stones weigh on average 20% of the bird's body mass, with an average of 230 stones per nest; the amount of stones vary with the size of the nest cavity, with larger cavities having more stones.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Warning |first1=Nathanial |last2=Benedict |first2=Lauryn |date=2016-03-01 |title=Facultative Nest Modification by Rock Wrens (Salpinctes obsoletus) |url=https://doi.org/10.3184/175815516X14447553481564 |journal=Avian Biology Research |language=EN |volume=9 |issue=1 |pages=58–65 |doi=10.3184/175815516X14447553481564 |issn=1758-1559|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

Nestlings leave the nest around 14{{En dash}}16 days old, moving to a different rock within {{Convert|75|m|ft|abbr=on}} from the nest, continuing to receive care from parents for about 1 week, and remained on the parent's territory for 4 more weeks. Nestlings molted at 28{{En dash}}35 days, however, they still possessed [[gape flange]]s.<ref name=":0"/>

=== Feeding<!-- done --> ===
[[File:Rock Wren (24384548040).jpg|left|thumb|A rock wren with a spider in its beak]]
Rock wrens are largely terrestrial, only flying for short durations, and instead using a very rapid gait to move around.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last=Tramontano |first=John Philip |date=1964 |title=Comparative studies of the Rock Wren and the Canyon Wren |url=https://repository.arizona.edu/bitstream/handle/10150/551742/AZU_TD_BOX250_E9791_1964_284.pdf |journal=University of Arizona}}</ref><ref name=":0" /> They eat [[insect]]s, such as [[leafhopper]]s, [[ant]]s, and [[weevil]]s; occasionally, other arthropods like [[spider]]s; and, rarely, [[seed]]s.<ref name=":2" /><!-- pages 30-32 --><ref name=":0" /> Rock wrens mainly forage during the day on open ground, rummaging the surface of rocks and soil and probing crevices.<ref name=":0" />

Nestlings were observed being fed grasshoppers, along with other insects; however, large portions of the prey could not be identified due to their small size.<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal |last=Pitt |first=Stephanie |date=2018-05-01 |title=Why Sing so Many Songs? Testing the Function of Song Type Repertoires in Rock Wrens Using Playback Experiments and Behavioral Observations |url=https://digscholarship.unco.edu/theses/314 |journal=Master's Theses}}</ref>

=== Vocalizations ===
{{Birdsong|species=Rock wren|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/salpinctes-obsoletus}}
Female rock wrens do not sing; however, males have large repertoires of 69–119 syllables, each song a 1–2.5 second trill of repeated syllables, with 3–5 seconds between songs.<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":0" /> Rock wrens have five call types: a two-part ''tic-ear/spit-tee'' sometimes used to warn of intruders; a rough trill, sometimes following a ''spit'', used as an alarm; an agitated chatter; and an aggressive ''k'chee'' used during confrontations.<ref name=":0" />

Rock wrens sing with a distinctive pattern intermediate between "eventual variety" (repeating one syllable before switching) and "immediate variety" (never repeating). A male typically sings each syllable three to four times within a burst of 10–15 syllables, rarely repeating the same syllable consecutively.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Kroodsma |first=Donald E. |date=1975 |title=Song Patterning in the Rock Wren |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1366225 |journal=The Condor |volume=77 |issue=3 |pages=294–303 |doi=10.2307/1366225 |jstor=1366225 |issn=0010-5422}}</ref> Song is primarily used for territorial defense, with males having greater repertoire size and pitch variety being more effective at deterring rivals.<ref name=":5" /><ref name=":0" />

== Conservation ==
The rock wren is a [[Least-concern species|least-concern]] species on the [[IUCN Red List]], with an estimated population of 3.4 million in the United States and Canada, with the global population being around 4.1 million.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Population Estimates Database – Partners in Flight Databases |url=https://pif.birdconservancy.org/population-estimates-database/ |access-date=2026-01-17 |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":0" /> However, the population is slowly declining, with a 13% decrease in population from 2010 to 2020.<ref>{{Cite journal |publisher=BirdLife International |date=2020-11-02 |title=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species: Salpinctes obsoletus |url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22711332/137628554 |journal=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250506060529/https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22711332/137628554 |archive-date=2025-05-06 |access-date=2025-12-29 |url-status=live }}</ref> The cause of this is unknown.<ref name=":3" /> Little is known about the causes of mortality or lifespan in adults,<ref name=":0" /> but nests are subject to predation.<ref name=":0" /> Observed nest predators included the [[striped racer]] snake, the [[desert woodrat]], and the [[white-tailed antelope squirrel]],<ref name=":0" /> as well as [[Pituophis catenifer|bullsnakes]] and [[Prairie Rattlesnake|prairie rattlesnakes]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Gobert Pitt |first1=Stephanie |last2=Benedict |first2=Lauryn |date=2022-06-01 |title=Breeding biology and reproductive success of Rock Wrens (Salpinctes obsoletus) in northern Colorado |url=https://doi.org/10.1676/21-00077 |journal=The Wilson Journal of Ornithology |volume=134 |issue=2 |pages=260–268 |doi=10.1676/21-00077 |issn=1559-4491 |url-access=subscription}}</ref> [[Brown-headed cowbird]]s are brood parasites of this species.<ref name=":0" />

==See also==
* [[Volcán Bárcena]], the volcano that caused the San Benedicto rock wren (a subspecies of ''Salpinctes'') to go extinct in 1952

zh.extract:
岩鹪鹩（台湾作普通岩鷦鷯，学名：Salpinctes obsoletus），是雀形目鹪鹩科的一种鸟类，属于单型的岩鹪鹩属（学名：Salpinctes）。
岩鹪鹩体重约16.37克，翼长约69毫米，喙宽度约2.9毫米，喙厚度约3.4毫米，嘴峰长约19.9毫米，跗蹠长约20.1毫米，尾长约55.6毫米。
岩鹪鹩是留鸟，栖息在岩石环境，它们是食肉动物，主要以昆虫、蜘蛛等陆生无脊椎动物为食。它们分布于加拿大南部、美国、墨西哥及中美洲。
